D7 Print L5 Evaluate Reference

Murray-Davies Dot Area

measure a tint's reflectance and the Murray-Davies formula returns its dot area — but it blames ink for darkening the paper itself does

A densitometer reads a tint's reflectance and Murray-Davies converts it to a fractional dot area against paper and solid. But part of a halftone's darkness is optical: light enters the paper, scatters under an ink dot, and is absorbed on the way out. Murray-Davies (which assumes no scatter) counts that optical gain as extra ink — so it overreads the real mechanical dot area.

The grey curve is the true mechanical dot area; the red curve is what Murray-Davies measures. Raise the paper's light scatter or the solid ink density and the gap widens — pure optical gain that the formula mistakes for fatter dots.
